Name: kernel_profiling_for_inference_optimization
Title: Profiling-Driven Kernel Optimization for Latency-Effective Deep Learning Inference
Short Hypothesis: By utilizing comprehensive kernel-level profiling combined with adaptive optimization strategies, we can achieve substantial reductions in inference latency on commodity GPUs, surpassing existing methods that focus on isolated optimizations.
Related Work: Existing literature primarily addresses specific optimization techniques like memory coalescing and mixed-precision arithmetic. While they provide speed improvements, they often do not consider the holistic impact of kernel profiling on system performance metrics. Our approach emphasizes profiling as a central component, allowing for adaptive optimizations based on real-time performance data across multiple layers and operations.
Abstract: Deep learning inference often suffers from latency bottlenecks, particularly on commodity GPUs where profiling and optimization strategies are not sufficiently integrated. This paper proposes a novel framework that combines kernel-level profiling with adaptive optimization techniques to enhance deep learning inference performance. We leverage detailed performance metrics obtained through profiling to inform on-the-fly optimizations, including dynamic memory management, kernel fusion, and precision tuning. By conducting rigorous A/B testing under fixed accuracy budgets, we demonstrate that our approach yields significant improvements in p50/p99 latency and tokens-per-second throughput. Our evaluations will be based on comprehensive benchmarks across various model architectures, ensuring reproducibility and practical applicability. This work aims to provide a concrete, hardware-aware optimization strategy that bridges the gap between theoretical models and practical deployment, ultimately advancing the state-of-the-art in efficient deep learning inference.
Experiments: 1. Implement a profiling tool to capture detailed metrics (occupancy, bandwidth, latency) for various kernel operations on commodity GPUs.
2. Develop an adaptive optimization algorithm that utilizes profiling data to dynamically adjust kernel configurations (e.g., tile sizes, precision) based on real-time performance metrics.

3. Conduct A/B tests comparing standard inference methods with our profiling-driven optimizations across different model architectures (e.g., CNNs, transformers) to evaluate latency (p50/p99) and throughput (tokens-per-second).
4. Measure energy consumption per token to assess the efficiency of the proposed optimizations.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed profiling tool might introduce performance overhead that could skew results if not properly managed.
Specific optimizations may not generalize well across different hardware configurations or GPU architectures.
Dependency on accurate profiling data may lead to challenges in real-time adaptation under varying workload conditions.
